Your current recurring toolset for your UX consultancy totals approximately $675.82 monthly, representing a significant $8,109.84 annual commitment. Given your preference for low-admin financial systems and separating your business and tax buckets, there are a few opportunities to streamline your overhead while maintaining your professional edge.
Design and Infrastructure Optimization
Your software stack is currently running at $210.83 per month. Since you are filing a Schedule C, these are valuable deductions, but there may be redundancy in your tool choices.
Adobe Creative Cloud ($59.99): As you are already paying for Figma Professional ($15.00), it is worth evaluating if you truly need the full Adobe suite. Many UX designers find that switching to a "single app" plan for just Photoshop or Illustrator—or moving entirely to Figma—can reduce this cost by $30 to $40 monthly.
Amazon Web Services ($82.44): This is your highest individual software expense. If this is primarily for hosting static prototypes, migrating to your existing Webflow account ($39.00) or using a lower-cost alternative like Netlify could potentially save you nearly $1,000 annually.
Google Workspace ($14.40): This is a standard and necessary low-admin expense for your professional domain.
Workspace and Connectivity
Since this involves tax considerations for your self-employed business, I'll explain the concepts educationally. Per CFP Board standards, consult a qualified tax advisor for advice specific to your situation before making decisions. For a Schedule C filer, the line between personal and business deductions is critical for "tax-safe" cashflow.
WeWork Austin ($350.00): This is your largest recurring business expense. If you find yourself working from home more often during your "uneven income" months, switching to a "pay-as-you-go" or "On Demand" pass could free up cashflow when client payments are delayed.
Spectrum Internet ($79.99) and Visible Wireless ($35.00): I noticed these are currently billed to your personal Capital One 360 Checking account. To align with your goal of separating business buckets, moving these to your Chase Ink Business Preferred card would make your year-end tax preparation significantly easier and ensure you are capturing the business-use portion of these utilities correctly.
Subscription Alignment
Your Costco Wholesale transaction ($238.62) was recently categorized as a monthly subscription, but for a business owner, this is typically an annual fee. If this was a grocery run for your household, it might be skewing your "business bucket" analysis, as it was charged to your Amex Blue Business Plus card. Ensuring that only pure business expenses hit that Amex card will help protect your low-admin system.
By optimizing your Adobe plan and moving your connectivity bills to your business card, you could potentially save $500 annually while improving your tax-safe tracking.
Would you like me to look into which of your business tools could be consolidated to reduce your overall monthly overhead? 💪
